title: Not all fats are created equal

chunks.title: Benefits of Animal Fats
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the preference for animal fats, highlighting the benefits of saturated fats like stearic acid, which positively influences mitochondrial function. They argue that a vegan diet lacks adequate sources of stearic acid, primarily found in animal fats, and that animal fats generally contain beneficial compounds such as odd-chain fatty acids.
chunks.transcript: in terms of fat you can see here if you're watching on YouTube I get 230 grams of fat most of that is coming from ground beef a good portion of that is coming from bone marrow and butter and whole milk so why do I prefer animal fats because I don't fear saturated fats and I want things like stearic acid an 18 carbon saturated fatty acid that I talked about in the past having benefits for the human organism there are studies with stearic acid where if you deprive people of stearic acid there are changes negative changes to the mitochondria which are reversed and you see the mitochondria turn on and do beta oxidation they start burning fat when stearic acid is reintroduced in the diet where vegans get stearic acid you're not getting any steering acid on a vegan diet unless you're eating cocoa butter that's the only place on a vegan diet that I'm aware of where there's any significant amount of stearic acid but animal fat the fat in 80 20 ground beef butter which also has butyric acid probably beneficial for the gut and butter and animal fat also have odd chain fatty acids something I've talked about in the past basically I much prefer animal fats to any other fats in

chunks.title: Concerns about Linoleic Acid
chunks.summary: The effects of linoleic acid in the diet are analyzed, with the speaker asserting that many olive oils contain high levels of linoleic acid, which could be detrimental to human health. They express a personal belief that keeping linoleic acid below 2% of caloric intake is optimal to avoid metabolic issues.

chunks.title: Cooking Methods and Preferences
chunks.summary: The speaker outlines their cooking methods, emphasizing a preference for grilling over using oils. They also mention that animal fats, like tallow and butter, are used minimally in their cooking, as most of the meat's natural fat suffices.
chunks.transcript: more or less of the linoleic acid things like grape seed and soybean oil are particularly rich in linoleic acid and are going to really push that up I don't want more linoleic acid in my diet I don't need olive oil in my diet I don't want more linoleic acid in my diet I don't need olive oil in my diet I would rather use animal fats I don't use any fats to cook I cook on a grill I've got a shrink Grill it's a stainless steel grill with a ceramic element on top and that's what I used to cook for my meat morning and evening I don't have a pan if I were going to use a pan it would be a stainless steel pan or a cast iron pan that I would put maybe a little bit of Tallow or butter in but most of the meat that I cook has enough fat in it that I don't even need to add oils to the pan so I don't use extra cooking oils and if I were they would be animal fats Tallow which is rendered beef fat or butter so those are the macro

chunks.title: Animal Protein Choices
chunks.summary: The speaker shares their preferences for different animal proteins, advocating for grass-fed beef while expressing caution about chicken due to its higher linoleic acid content when raised on corn and soy. They also touch on concerns regarding pork and encourage viewers to prioritize high-quality sources.
chunks.transcript: breakdown let's talk a little bit about the foods I eat and then we'll get into some of the micronutrients I prefer to eat beef because I can get it grass-fed and grass finished I don't like to eat chicken but listen chicken is way better than plant-based meat and if you can get chicken and you like chicken then eat chicken my concern with chicken is that chicken is a monogastric animal like a pig and that if you are eating chicken fat from an animal that's eating lots of corn and soy you're pushing the amount of linoleic acid up in your diet and that can stall weight loss for some people so if you are at a healthy weight if you're at the body composition you want eat chicken eat pork eat bacon I'm fine with that obviously get the best sources you can they are animal Foods they're way better than the other options but if you're not losing weight

chunks.title: Fish and Heavy Metal Concerns
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses the benefits of fish but warns against the accumulation of heavy metals and microplastics in fish, leading to a preference for land animals. They note that while fish can be healthier than processed foods, moderation is key.
chunks.transcript: understand that getting rid of chicken fat or pork fat in your diet I believe may help you long term this is just what I do but you can be much more Broad in your scope if you want depending what works for you I also don't have fish in my diet but Fish can be great select low mercury fish salmon or smaller fish don't eat it a ton don't make fish the only part of your diet if you eat a lot of fish be sure to check your heavy metals I don't eat fish because of microplastics because of pfas's parafluoroalkylated substances that accumulate in fish probably more than land animals and because of the heavy metals but listen fish is way better in my opinion than processed food and Fishes gasp way better than vegetables I believe so if you want to include fish

chunks.title: Effects of Olive Oil on Weight Loss
chunks.summary: There is a discussion on the role of olive oil in weight loss, suggesting that for some, removing it from the diet might enhance efforts to shed pounds by reducing linoleic acid intake. The speaker presents research showing weight loss correlations with reduced linoleic acid consumption.
